category_title: Students who talk out to access peer attention
minimal_context: Craig is in the general classroom during independent work time, expected to complete his assigned tasks.
dialogue:
TUTOR: It is time for independent work. Please start working quietly on your assigned tasks.

STUDENT: (Turns to a peer) Hey, did you see the game last night?

TUTOR: Craig, remember the rules for independent work time.

STUDENT: I am just asking a question. (Turns back to peer) Seriously though, what did you think of that last play?

TUTOR: Please focus on your own assignment.

STUDENT: (Laughs as peer responds back) I know, right? It was crazy! Look at this drawing I did of it.

options.A: Pat Craig on the shoulder, smile, and ask him to complete another assigned task, offering this brief physical and social interaction while he remains at his desk during independent work time.
options.B: Respond to the student by providing enthusiastic verbal praise, saying, 'Another one already? No way! I’m so proud of you! Good job, keep going,' during the independent work time.
options.C: Provide free access to a textured sensory ball, allowing him to keep the item at his desk while he continues working on his assigned tasks during the independent work time.
options.D: Precorrect the expected behavior with reminders to follow the talk/no talk policy, using the color-coded signs to indicate when it is acceptable to talk (green) and when the expectation is to be quiet (red).
